full_response:
Step 1.  Rewrite the objective in a single variable  
Let  

  s := x_A + x_B              (0 ≤ s ≤ 1 because the link must not be overloaded)

When s ≤ 1 the given utilities are  

 u_A = x_A(1 − s), u_B = x_B(1 − s).

Hence the total utility is  

 U(s) = u_A + u_B = (x_A + x_B)(1 − s) = s(1 − s).

Observe that U depends only on the sum s, not on the individual split between Alice and Bob.

Step 2.  Maximize U(s) on the feasible interval 0 ≤ s ≤ 1  
U(s) = s − s² is a concave quadratic.  
Take the derivative: U′(s) = 1 − 2s.  
Set U′(s)=0 ⇒ 1 − 2s = 0 ⇒ s* = ½.  
Second derivative U″(s)=−2<0 confirms this is the unique maximum.

Thus the maximal total utility is  

 U_max = U(½) = ½·(1 − ½) = ¼.

Step 3.  Choose any x_A, x_B that sum to ½  
Any pair with x_A ≥ 0, x_B ≥ 0 and x_A + x_B = ½ attains the optimum.  
A natural symmetric choice is  

 x_A = ¼, x_B = ¼.

Then  
 u_A = ¼·½ = ⅛, u_B = ¼·½ = ⅛, total = ¼ (the maximum).

\boxed{\text{Any actions with }x_A+x_B=\tfrac12\text{ (e.g.\ }x_A=x_B=\tfrac14\text{)} give the maximal total utility } 

Confidence: 0.78
extracted_answer: \text{Any actions with }x_A+x_B=\tfrac12\text{ (e.g.\ }x_A=x_B=\tfrac14\text{)} give the maximal total utility
